title: Colours
content: Colours make art beautiful. Red, blue and yellow are primary colours. When you mix two primary colours you get a secondary colour. Mixing red and blue gives purple.
question: What are red, blue and yellow called?

title: Painting
content: Painting is a way to make art. We use brushes and paint. Different brushes make different marks. Thin brushes make fine lines. Wide brushes make big strokes.
question: What do thin brushes make?

title: Drawing
content: Drawing is using a pencil, pen or crayon to make pictures. We can draw from life or from our imagination. Sketching is a quick way to practise drawing.
question: What is sketching?
options: A quick way to practise drawing
answer: A quick way to practise drawing

title: Music Rhythm
content: Rhythm is the beat in music. You can feel the rhythm when you tap your foot. Fast rhythm makes music feel exciting. Slow rhythm makes music feel calm.
question: How does slow rhythm make music feel?

title: Musical Instruments
content: There are four families of instruments. String instruments like violin make sound by vibrating strings. Wind instruments like flute use air. Brass instruments like trumpet use buzzing lips. Percussion instruments like drums are hit.
question: What family does a violin belong to?

title: Drama
content: Drama is acting out stories. Actors use their voice and body to show feelings. Drama helps us understand how other people feel. We perform in plays and shows.
question: What do actors use to show feelings?

title: Warm and Cool Colours
content: Warm colours are red, orange and yellow. They remind us of fire and sunshine. Cool colours are blue, green and purple. They remind us of water and sky. Artists choose colours to create mood.
question: What do warm colours remind us of?

title: Sculpture
content: Sculpture is three dimensional art. Sculptors work with clay, stone, wood or metal. The finished work can be touched as well as seen. Famous sculptures include the Statue of Liberty.
question: How many dimensions does a sculpture have?

title: The Colour Wheel
content: The colour wheel shows how colours relate to each other. Primary colours are spread around it. Secondary colours sit between the primary colours. Complementary colours are opposite each other.
question: Where do complementary colours sit on the colour wheel?

title: Storytelling in Art
content: Art can tell stories. Cave paintings showed hunting scenes. Medieval tapestries told tales of battles and heroes. Comic strips tell stories with pictures and words.
question: What did cave paintings often show?

title: Abstract Art
content: Abstract art does not show real things. It uses shapes, colours and lines to express ideas and feelings. Wassily Kandinsky was a famous abstract artist. People feel different emotions looking at abstract art.
question: What does abstract art use to express feelings?

title: Portraits
content: A portrait is a picture of a person. Artists paint or draw the face to show character. The Mona Lisa is the most famous portrait. Leonardo da Vinci painted it.
question: What is a portrait?

title: Impressionism
content: Impressionism is an art style from France. Artists like Monet painted outdoors. They used short brushstrokes to capture light and movement. Impressionist paintings look soft and blurry up close.
question: What did Impressionists like to capture?

title: Texture in Art
content: Texture is how something feels or appears to feel. Rough, smooth, bumpy and soft are types of texture. Artists create texture using different tools and materials. Impasto painting uses thick paint to create texture.
question: What is impasto painting?
options: Using thick paint to create texture
answer: Using thick paint to create texture

title: Pop Art
content: Pop art uses images from everyday life. Andy Warhol was a famous pop artist. He made art from soup cans and celebrity faces. Pop art is colourful, bold and fun.
question: Who was a famous pop artist?

title: Typography
content: Typography is the art of arranging letters. Different fonts have different styles. Serif fonts have small tails on the letters. Sans-serif fonts do not. Good typography makes text easy to read.
question: What are the small tails on serif fonts called?

title: Animation
content: Animation creates moving images. Each image is slightly different from the last. When shown quickly they appear to move. Traditional animation was drawn by hand. Now it is often done on computers.
question: What makes animation appear to move?

title: Collage
content: A collage is made by cutting and sticking. Different materials like paper, fabric and photos are used. Henri Matisse made beautiful cut-out collages. Collage is a fun way to make art.
question: What materials can be used in a collage?

title: Street Art
content: Street art appears in public spaces. Banksy is a famous anonymous street artist. Street art can be political, humorous or beautiful. Some is welcomed, some causes controversy.
question: Who is a famous anonymous street artist?

title: The Elements of Art
content: The elements of art are the building blocks of all artwork. They include line, shape, colour, texture, form, space and tone. Artists combine these elements to create their work.
question: Which of these is an element of art?

title: Photography
content: Photography captures images using a camera. The lens focuses light onto a sensor or film. Good photographers think about composition, light and subject. Photography is used in art, journalism and science.
question: What does the camera lens do?

title: Music Notes
content: Music is written using notes on a stave. A crotchet lasts one beat. A minim lasts two beats. A semibreve lasts four beats. Notes tell musicians how long to hold each sound.
question: How long does a crotchet last?

title: Cave Paintings
content: Cave paintings are the oldest known art. They were made over 40,000 years ago. Early humans painted animals, hands and hunting scenes. The Lascaux caves in France are famous for their paintings.
question: How old are the oldest cave paintings?

title: Perspective
content: Perspective creates the illusion of depth in a 2D drawing. Objects that are far away appear smaller. Lines appear to converge at a vanishing point. Artists use perspective to make scenes look real.
question: Why do distant objects appear smaller?

title: Ceramics
content: Ceramics is the art of making objects from clay. Clay is shaped, dried and then fired in a kiln. The heat makes the clay hard and permanent. Pottery, tiles and sculptures are types of ceramics.
question: What makes clay hard and permanent?

title: Ancient Egyptian Art
content: Ancient Egyptians made beautiful art. They decorated temples and tombs. Figures were drawn in a special flat style. The pharaoh was always shown biggest to show importance.
question: Why was the pharaoh always shown biggest?

title: Cubism
content: Cubism was invented by Picasso and Braque. It shows objects from many angles at once. Cubist paintings look like they have been broken into pieces. It changed art forever.
question: Who invented Cubism?

title: Digital Art
content: Digital art is created using computers, tablets and software. Artists use tools like Adobe Photoshop or Procreate. Digital art can be paintings, illustrations or animation. It is easy to undo mistakes.
question: What is one advantage of digital art?

title: The Orchestra
content: An orchestra is a large group of musicians. They are divided into sections. String, woodwind, brass and percussion. The conductor stands at the front and leads the musicians.
question: Who leads an orchestra?

title: Printmaking
content: Printmaking is creating images from a prepared surface. A design is cut into a block. Ink is applied and paper is pressed onto it. Multiple copies can be made from one block.
question: What is applied to the block in printmaking?

title: Mosaics
content: Mosaics are made from small pieces of coloured glass, stone or tile. They were popular in ancient Rome and Greece. Famous mosaics decorate churches and palaces. Mosaic making requires great patience.
question: What are mosaics made from?
options: Small coloured pieces of glass or stone
answer: Small coloured pieces of glass or stone

title: Graffiti Art
content: Graffiti art is found on walls and surfaces in public spaces. Some see it as vandalism. Others see it as powerful art. Keith Haring used graffiti to spread positive messages.
question: What did Keith Haring use graffiti to do?

title: Architecture
content: Architecture is the art and science of designing buildings. Architects think about beauty and function. They consider light, space and materials. Famous buildings include the Eiffel Tower and the Taj Mahal.
question: What do architects think about?

title: Surrealism
content: Surrealism creates dreamlike and strange images. Salvador Dali was a famous surrealist artist. His painting of melting clocks is famous. Surrealism was inspired by dreams and the unconscious mind.
question: Who is a famous Surrealist artist?

title: Weaving
content: Weaving is a craft that creates fabric. Threads are passed over and under each other on a loom. Different colours create patterns. Weaving has been done for thousands of years.
question: What is used to weave fabric?

title: The Renaissance
content: The Renaissance was a great period of art in Europe. Artists studied human anatomy to draw bodies accurately. Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo and Raphael were famous Renaissance artists.
question: What did Renaissance artists study to draw bodies accurately?

title: Graphic Design
content: Graphic designers create visual communication. They design logos, posters, books and websites. They use colour, type and images together. Good graphic design is clear and attractive.
question: What do graphic designers create?

title: Music and Emotions
content: Music can make us feel different emotions. Fast, loud music can make us feel energetic. Slow, quiet music can make us feel calm or sad. Composers choose notes carefully to create feelings.
question: How does fast, loud music make us feel?

title: The Colour Purple
content: Purple is a secondary colour. It is made by mixing red and blue. In history, purple dye was very expensive. It was worn by royalty and important people. Purple is often associated with royalty.
question: Why was purple historically associated with royalty?

title: Stage Design
content: Stage design creates the look of a theatre show. Sets, lighting and costumes work together. Stage designers read the script first. They discuss ideas with the director. The design helps the audience understand the story.
question: What does the stage designer read first?

title: Folk Art
content: Folk art is made by ordinary people, not professional artists. It reflects the culture and traditions of a community. It includes crafts, textiles and decorative art. It is passed down through generations.
question: Who makes folk art?

title: Drawing Proportions
content: Proportions describe the size of one part compared to another. In a typical face, the eyes are halfway down the head. The nose is halfway between eyes and chin. Knowing proportions helps artists draw realistic faces.
question: Where are the eyes in proportion to the head?

title: Light in Art
content: Artists study how light falls on objects. The lightest part is called the highlight. The darkest part is the shadow. Areas between are called midtones. This creates a sense of form.
question: What is the lightest part of an object called?

title: Batik
content: Batik is a fabric dyeing art from Indonesia. Wax is applied to cloth in patterns. When the cloth is dyed, wax prevents the colour from soaking in. The wax is removed to reveal the pattern.
question: What does wax do in batik?

title: Origami
content: Origami is the Japanese art of paper folding. A single sheet of paper is folded into shapes. No cutting or glue is used. Traditional shapes include cranes, frogs and boats.
question: What is origami?

title: Music Technology
content: Music technology uses electronics to create sound. Synthesisers create electronic sounds. Recording software captures and edits music. DJs mix music using technology. It has transformed modern music.
question: What does recording software do?

title: Graphic Novels
content: Graphic novels tell stories through pictures and words. They are like long comic books. Characters and backgrounds are drawn in detail. Many famous novels have been turned into graphic novels.
question: How do graphic novels tell stories?

title: Colour Psychology
content: Colour affects how we feel. Red can feel exciting or dangerous. Blue can feel calm or sad. Yellow feels cheerful and bright. Companies choose their logo colours carefully because of colour psychology.
question: How might red make us feel?

title: Performing Arts
content: Performing arts include theatre, dance and music. Performers entertain an audience. They rehearse many times before performing. Performing takes courage and lots of practice.
question: What do performing arts include?

title: Van Gogh
content: Vincent van Gogh was a Dutch painter. He painted bold and colourful pictures. His most famous work is The Starry Night. Van Gogh used swirling brushstrokes to show movement.
question: What painting is Van Gogh famous for?

title: Monet's Water Lilies
content: Claude Monet painted a series of water lily paintings. He painted the pond in his garden at Giverny. The paintings show reflections of light on water. They are a perfect example of Impressionism.
question: Where did Monet paint his water lilies?

title: Calligraphy
content: Calligraphy is the art of beautiful handwriting. Different scripts include Arabic, Chinese and Roman. A special pen with a broad nib is often used. Calligraphy is used in invitations, signage and religious texts.
question: What is calligraphy?

title: Musical Scales
content: A musical scale is a set of notes in order. The C major scale has notes C, D, E, F, G, A and B. Major scales sound bright and happy. Minor scales sound sadder and darker.
question: How does a C major scale sound?

title: Shadow Puppets
content: Shadow puppets are figures held behind a lit screen. Their shadows are seen by the audience. This art form is thousands of years old. It is popular in Indonesia and China.
question: Where is shadow puppetry popular?

title: Landscape Art
content: Landscape paintings show outdoor scenes. Mountains, rivers, forests and seas are common subjects. Artists try to capture the beauty of nature. Turner was a famous British landscape painter.
question: What are common subjects in landscape art?

title: Dali's Clocks
content: Salvador Dali painted The Persistence of Memory. It shows melting clocks in a desert landscape. It represents how time feels different in dreams. It is one of the most recognised paintings in the world.
question: What does The Persistence of Memory show?

title: Body Percussion
content: Body percussion means making music with your body. Clapping, stomping, clicking fingers and patting knees all create rhythms. It is used in music education worldwide. No instruments are needed.
question: What does body percussion use?

title: Fabric Arts
content: Fabric arts include sewing, knitting and embroidery. Fabric can be cut and sewn into clothes. Knitting uses yarn to make fabric. Embroidery adds decorative stitches to fabric.
question: What does knitting use to make fabric?

title: Colour Mixing Experiment
content: When you mix paint, you get new colours. Mixing red and yellow gives orange. Mixing all three primary colours gives brown. Adding white to any colour makes a tint. Adding black makes a shade.
question: What do you make when you add white to a colour?

title: Stage Lighting
content: Stage lighting helps create the mood of a performance. Red light can suggest danger. Blue can suggest night or sadness. Spotlights focus on one actor. Good lighting can transform a scene.
question: What can red stage lighting suggest?

title: Musical Theatre
content: Musical theatre combines songs, acting and dance. Famous musicals include The Lion King and Grease. Performers must be able to sing, dance and act. Musicals have big sets and costumes.
question: What must performers in musicals be able to do?

title: Lino Printing
content: Lino printing is a type of printmaking. A design is cut into a flat piece of linoleum. Ink is rolled over it. Paper is pressed on to transfer the design. Many prints can be made from one block.
question: What is the surface used in lino printing?

title: Cultural Art
content: Every culture has its own art traditions. Aboriginal Australians make dot paintings. Native Americans weave colourful rugs. Japanese artists make delicate woodblock prints. Art connects us to our heritage.
question: What type of paintings do Aboriginal Australians make?

title: Still Life
content: A still life painting shows objects that do not move. Common objects include fruit, flowers, vases and books. The artist carefully arranges the objects. Still life helps artists practise observation skills.
question: What objects are common in still life paintings?

title: Pointillism
content: Pointillism was invented by Georges Seurat. He painted using tiny dots of pure colour. When seen from far away, the dots blend together. A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte is his famous painting.
question: Who invented Pointillism?

title: Musical Dynamics
content: Dynamics describe the loudness of music. Fortissimo means very loud. Forte means loud. Piano means soft. Pianissimo means very soft. Dynamics help make music expressive.
question: What does fortissimo mean?

title: Improvisation in Drama
content: Improvisation means performing without a script. Actors must think quickly. They listen and respond to each other. Improvisation builds confidence and creativity. It is used in warm up exercises.
question: What is improvisation in drama?

title: Art Therapy
content: Art therapy uses art to support mental health. Drawing and painting can help express feelings. It is used in hospitals and schools. Art therapy helps people deal with stress and trauma.
question: What does art therapy help people do?
options: Express feelings and deal with stress
answer: Express feelings and deal with stress

title: Wax Resist
content: Wax resist is a creative art technique. Wax crayon is drawn onto paper. Then watercolour paint is brushed over it. The paint does not stick where the wax is. This creates interesting effects.
question: What happens when paint is applied over wax crayon?
options: It does not stick to the wax
answer: It does not stick to the wax

title: Symbolism in Art
content: Artists often use symbols to add meaning. A dove represents peace. A skull can represent death. In religious art, a halo represents holiness. Understanding symbols helps us interpret art.
question: What does a dove represent in art?

title: Body Language in Drama
content: Body language communicates without words. Standing tall shows confidence. Slouching shows sadness or tiredness. Crossed arms can show anger. Actors use body language to develop their characters.
question: What does standing tall show?

title: Percussion Family
content: Percussion instruments are hit or shaken to make sound. Drums, cymbals and xylophones are percussion. Some have musical pitch, like a xylophone. Others just make rhythm, like a bass drum.
question: Which of these is a percussion instrument?

title: Mondrian
content: Piet Mondrian was a Dutch artist. He used only primary colours and black and white. He painted grids of rectangles. His work influenced graphic design and fashion. It is very bold and simple.
question: What colours did Mondrian use?
options: Only primary colours, black and white
answer: Only primary colours, black and white

title: Documentary Photography
content: Documentary photography tells stories about real life. It records people, events and places. War photographers work in dangerous conditions. Their images can change history.
question: What does documentary photography record?
options: People, events and places in real life
answer: People, events and places in real life

title: African Art
content: African art has a rich history. Masks are central to many African cultures. They are used in ceremonies and dances. African art has influenced many modern artists including Picasso.
question: Who was influenced by African art?

title: Op Art
content: Op Art stands for Optical Art. It creates illusions that trick the eye. Patterns of lines and shapes seem to move or vibrate. Bridget Riley is a famous Op Art artist.
question: What does Op Art do?
options: Creates illusions that trick the eye
answer: Creates illusions that trick the eye

title: The Human Figure in Art
content: Drawing the human figure is a key skill in art. Artists learn about proportions, movement and posture. Life drawing is when an artist draws a model. It takes many hours of practice.
question: What is life drawing?
options: Drawing a model in real life
answer: Drawing a model in real life

title: Choral Music
content: A choir is a group of singers. They sing in different parts. Soprano is the highest voice. Bass is the lowest. Singing in harmony creates a beautiful blended sound.
question: Which is the highest vocal part in a choir?

title: Narrative Art
content: Narrative art tells a story. Medieval illustrated manuscripts told religious stories. Today, graphic novels tell modern stories. Art has always been a way to share tales across generations.
question: What did medieval illustrated manuscripts tell?

title: Art Movements
content: An art movement is a group of artists who share ideas. Impressionism, Surrealism and Pop Art are movements. Each has its own style and philosophy. Movements shape the history of art.
question: What is an art movement?
options: A group of artists who share ideas
answer: A group of artists who share ideas

title: Michelangelo
content: Michelangelo was an Italian Renaissance artist. He painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling in Rome. He was also a great sculptor. His statue of David is world famous.
question: What famous ceiling did Michelangelo paint?

title: Sound Layers in Music
content: Music can have many layers of sound. A bass guitar plays low notes underneath. Drums keep the rhythm. A singer carries the melody on top. These layers together create a rich sound.
question: What does the singer carry in music?

title: Printmaking Techniques
content: There are different types of printmaking. Relief printing cuts away areas of a block. Intaglio printing incises lines into metal. Screen printing pushes ink through a mesh. Each gives a unique effect.
question: What does screen printing push ink through?

title: The Artist's Toolkit
content: Artists use many different tools. Pencils draw fine lines. Charcoal makes bold marks. Watercolour paints are transparent. Acrylic paints dry quickly and can be mixed with water.
question: What is special about watercolour paints?

title: Art in Advertising
content: Advertisements use art to sell products. Designers choose colours, fonts and images carefully. The goal is to catch the eye and create desire. Art and commerce work together in advertising.
question: What is the goal of advertising art?
options: To catch the eye and create desire
answer: To catch the eye and create desire

title: Contemporary Art
content: Contemporary art is made in the present day. It uses new materials and ideas. Some installations fill entire rooms. Contemporary art challenges our thinking and can be controversial.
question: What can contemporary art challenge?

title: Book Illustration
content: Book illustrators create images for books. They help bring stories to life. Picture books combine images and words. Illustrators must understand the story deeply before they can draw.
question: What must illustrators understand before drawing?

title: Japanese Art
content: Japanese art includes woodblock printing, ceramics and gardens. Hokusai painted The Great Wave. Japanese artists value simplicity and nature. Zen philosophy has influenced Japanese art.
question: What did Hokusai paint?

title: Music Around the World
content: Every culture has its own music. Indian classical music uses a raga. African music uses complex rhythms. Blues music came from African American culture in the USA. Music connects people.
question: Where did Blues music come from?
options: African American culture in the USA
answer: African American culture in the USA

title: Fashion Design
content: Fashion designers create clothes and accessories. They sketch designs, choose fabrics and create patterns. Fashion combines art and function. Famous fashion houses include Chanel and Gucci.
question: What do fashion designers create?

title: Aboriginal Art
content: Aboriginal Australians use dot painting to record stories. The dots represent journeys, landscapes and animals. The symbols have sacred meaning. It is one of the oldest art traditions on Earth.
question: What do dots represent in Aboriginal art?

title: Stage Performance
content: A stage performance requires many people. Actors learn their lines. Directors guide the acting. Set designers build the stage. Costumiers make the clothes. The lighting crew controls the lights.
question: What do costumiers do?

title: Colour in Nature
content: Nature is full of colour. Flowers use colour to attract bees. Chameleons change colour for camouflage. The rainbow shows all seven colours of light. Artists take inspiration from nature's colours.
question: Why do flowers have bright colours?

title: World Architecture
content: Famous buildings represent their cultures. The Eiffel Tower is French. The Colosseum is Roman. The Taj Mahal is Indian. Each building tells a story about its time and culture.
question: What country is the Taj Mahal from?

title: Sound and Silence
content: Music is not just sound. Silence is equally important. A pause can create drama and suspense. Rest notes in music show when to be silent. The space between sounds gives music its shape.
question: What do rest notes in music show?

title: Sketchbooks
content: Artists keep sketchbooks for ideas and practice. They draw what they see around them. Sketches do not have to be perfect. A sketchbook is a private space to experiment and explore.
question: What is an artist's sketchbook used for?

title: Batik Art
content: Batik is a wax-resist dyeing technique. It comes from Indonesia. A tool called a tjanting applies hot wax. When dyed, the waxed areas resist colour. Batik creates beautiful patterned fabrics.
question: Where does batik art come from?

title: Symmetry in Art
content: Symmetry means both sides of something look the same. Mandalas are perfectly symmetrical. Many buildings use symmetry. Artists sometimes break symmetry on purpose to create interest.
question: What is a mandala?

title: Art Critique
content: Art critique is the analysis and judgement of art. We describe what we see, analyse the techniques used, and share our interpretation and evaluation. Art critique helps us understand and appreciate art more deeply.
question: What does art critique help us do?
options: Understand and appreciate art more deeply
answer: Understand and appreciate art more deeply

title: The Joy of Creating
content: Creating art makes people happy. It allows us express ourselves. Everyone can be creative in their own way. Art does not need to be perfect. The joy is in the process of making.
question: What is the most important part of making art?
